The text is a heavily garbled rendering of the famous **Farewell Address of George Washington (1796)** – the speech he delivered as he stepped down from the Presidency.  I have restored the original meaning, punctuation, and paragraph breaks, and then added short notes to clarify the points he was making.

---

## 1. Opening – Recollection of the Nation’s Call

> **“In the address which I shall now close, I will take the liberty to add a perspective that may, perhaps, convey some useful lessons to the public you represent – lessons drawn from the conduct of my life.”**

*Washington is reminding his listeners that his final words will contain reflections on what he has learned from his own term in office.*

> **“As the circumstances that bind a dutiful office to the grateful support of the people, which my fellow‑countrymen have twice bestowed upon me, I cannot but express a sincere desire that this service may end without the imposition of any duty that could be construed as a mere burden.”**

*He notes the “two‑term” tradition (the people have chosen him twice) and stresses that he sees his office as a duty, not a burdensome imposition.*

**Address on the Decision to Retire**

Friends and fellow citizens,

After long and careful contemplation, I have come to the reluctant conclusion that it is time for me to retire from public service.  This decision has been forced upon me not by any waning of zeal for the nation's welfare, but by the inevitable march of time and the need to devote my remaining energies to private affairs and personal reflection.

My attachment to this office is inviolable; every day I have served has only strengthened the conviction that the Constitution must be guarded, that the union of the states must endure, and that the republic must continue to prosper.  Yet I have also seen how easily public confidence can be shaken and how fragile the foundations of trust become when they are left unattended.
